review: Its not the best game in the whole world but it keeps me entertained.  I dont like taking care of Pals anymore so I just dont do that anymore.  I basically just collect materials etc and build houses.  I love that I can get quite creative if I put my mind to it and I like that it is not strongly quest driven so I feel free to do as I please and I LOVE that I dont have to play with people.  I also like that I dont have to pay for anything.  So sick and tired of purchasing crap from other games and then having to delete the game later because it turns out it sucks.  There are issues with building in this game but hey no game is perfect.

Palworld is a nice little game. It's ARK with Pokemon. I bought the game because I heard many great things about it, and I remember people loving the game when it first launched.

Unfortunately, I have quite a few problems with the game. IMO there are a lot of baffling decisions when it comes to the difficulty, even if "oh it's fine though cause you can change it at will". I find this is still worthy of criticism because the default settings themselves don't feel balanced, so you need to tweak things slowly until you find what works for you. That, or you look up an online guide that says "these are the best palworld settings for maximum fun!" but fun is subjective...

The music is perfectly mediocre. Not much else to say about it lol

Quests are a nice little checkmark, but the lack of any real interconnected quest or side story makes it feel empty. It would've been nice if the game had quest chains with written stories, but nah, they're all individual.

The "main story" or "main objective" to defeat the towers is... serviceable. The unfortunate thing is that the game doesn't eventually develop into a mood of "you're now in the final area! You've almost beaten the game!". Things just feel the same, all the time. The only difference is that now you can unlock things in the upgrade tree... yay? I've yet to defeat the Feybreak Tower, but part of me believes me that the game will not have any closure or sense of finality once I do that. You may argue that the game is still in early access.. but c'mon. That's just cope.

The combat itself is really whatever. Player interactivity feels practically absent: Because Player weapons feel so weak in general, the only thing you're left to do in combat is use items, switch Pals, and dodge attacks. This makes Palworld feel more like an auto-clicker/afk.

One more problem I can point out is the grinding is so monotonous in the late game. Yes, change the settings... But if your stuff is underleveled, there's isn't a fun way to level up your Pals if you're trying to get from 60 to 65. This problem is highlighted even more when you realize the combat is just a dodge/switch Pal loop.

--

For the positives though, and why Palworld may be good for you..
- You like optimizing teams
- You like exploring
- The game genuinely feels like an "Open world Pokemon game" at times
- You like breeding
- You like collecting 
- You like optimizing workflow in a factory setting

To conclude, I've had my fun with the game, but I find it hard to believe there isn't a better game out there than what Palworld has to offer. The fact that I got mislead by the people who called it "Pokemon with guns" when it's actually "ARK with a pokemon mod" feels saddening, because I would've thought the game had more stuff to do with the Pals other than labor.  

You could say that "Palworld just isn't for me", and that might be true. I do like my games to have a sense of direction and finality to them, like how you can still explore and do things in Elden Ring after you've defeated the final boss (which you know is the final boss due to the setup).

At the very least, in terms of the Price per Playtime, Palworld is definitely worth buying.

Until you reach the 5th/6th tower the game is a lot of fun. After that it becomes an absolute slog of grinding and frustration. Mid to late game bosses have way too much health and deal way too much damage to the point where if you bring a full team that counters the bosses element they will be one shot anyway. Materials needed for late game gear have absurd crafting costs that take way too long farm for (the strongest weapon currently is not even worth crafting because the amount of materials needed to craft the ammo is ridiculous). I wish I could recommend it as the first half of the game is genuinely well paced but after that it just becomes monotonous.

Very fun, entertainingly quirky. It's like a love-letter to all the survival/building games out there, and honestly for all the obvious appearance comparisons out there...

For all the "off-brand Pokewhatever" vibe, I pick up a lot of the Pals being far more reminiscent of some of the mid-to-endgame dinos from Ark (Reptyro, I'm lookin' at you, you're the Ankylo's anime cousin.)

This is perfectly playable in early access, and they keep adding free content -- collabs and additions that aren't doing that "pay for this DLC before the game is out of early access" nonsense thus far.

It runs far better than and has way less jank in base building than Ark EVER HAS. Thus far my experience is the bugs are few and far between. The dungeons and boss instances are a little repetitive but fun.

The only real issue I have is while working through the main quest sometimes there's a real jump, you get told "go beat this boss in this place" and they're waaaaay tougher than the previous boss, so you have to take time and build up when you didn't expect it.

I wish there was a Mixed review. Palworld is a survival game with a skin of a Monster Collector. It's a nice fresh breath of air for people playing for the Monster Collector genre aspect, It got a lot of traction because people got tired of same old Pokemon formula in Monster collector genre. However, in reality, it brings nothing new into the survival genre itself. People who are coming from the survival genre will find the exact same reskin of concepts of grind and automation. Adventure is meh as the world itself is empty and bland. Dungeons are boring, and everything else about the game including quests are undercooked except the Pal and automation mechanics. Same survival dna where grind gets worse and more time-consuming near-endgame. This game will offer you nothing new, if you are coming from other survival games and looking for a game that does different within the genre. If you are a monster collector fan, game will feel fun until you hit bigger grinds that are part of survival games and you might be turned off by it.

Just got the 57/57 achievements as of May 5, 2026.  So, I think I can give a full review.

I returned to this game after a two year break--I basically jumped on the hype train when it came out, and then it faded away.  It felt very early access back then.

Returning to this game in 2026, the polish is there and this game shines. It works for both casual players and those who want to min-max.  The QoL improvements from when I last played are staggering. One includes a chest that allows you to store a set number of items that are shared amongst bases--similar to an Ender Chest from Minecraft.  While I'm not sure if this was present in 2024, the global palbox prevents you from running out of storage with your Pals--provided you do not breed 10,000. Expanded machinery and a money printer later in the game really help you keep the pace of this game.

That's where I heap on the praise.  A lot of survival games have a great start, and a terrible middle, and a lackluster end. I'm looking at you, Studio Wildcard.  Palworld has masterful pacing in a survival crafting game.  There is some RNG, but it's not abominable and you can beat the bosses and the game without having to grind for the absolutely best gear.  The additions of Sukijima--which added Japanese architecture, is a crown jewel of this game that does indeed have an Albernueric farm.

I was watching a guy review Warhammer 40k, and he said "When something becomes derivative enough, it eventually swings back to being original."  That's how this game feels.  You have things torn from other games like:

- Evergaols, spirit springs and somehow Godfrey from Elden Ring
- the obvious monster catching that Nintendon't ripped off from another game
- the crafting mechanics of Ark without the janky build mechanics
- the BotW scope of the map
- odd lore that somehow feels like it was inspired by Adventure Time?

But when you mix all these ingredients together, you get a great game.  Some games, when I finish them, I feel glad it's over.  This is one of those games where I'm glad it existed.

The premise i quite enjoyed. I was an avid Pokemon player from Diamond to White to Pokemon Y.

To me there is two sides of this fence, me who was a Pokemon fan and was tired after Pokemon Y that they were releasing the same formula year after year where most of us wanted pokemon in a different genre.
To me, Palworld scratched that itch like Pixelmon did where Gamefreak didn't dare to adventure. On the other side is my friend who like me was brought up with Pokemon but due to the nature of it being Chinese owned, in their eyes a "blatant ripoff" and the Pals are unoriginal/uninteresting....

To me, i enjoy the pals, some are like early level pokemon like bidoof and pidgey, some appear as pseudo-bosses.

The gameplay is refreshing, gliding around the map until you can get a pal that can fly quickly. Mixture of using a Pal to fight and using your own weapons to fight off wild pals. Some Pals are passive unless attacked and some attack on sight.

There are 120 or so pals, some are "ignis" varients usually mirroring the type, so if one was blue coloured and ice type, the other is pink and fire type and i quite like this for in my mind "shinies with type variation"
On Shinies, there are ones that glow in the wild, often shining and being overleveled from their normal level. There are also pseudo-bosses of normal pals you can get that are "oversized" which is cool too.

The building mechanic exists, i haven't used it for base building all that much but seems cool if not a bit weird when the ground is uneven. 

The crafting for foods, resource refining, etc is quite entertaining to make an efficient workhorse of a base with work pals.

Leveling up it doesn't explain that catching 10-20 of new pals gives you a massive exp boost that fighting doesn't deliver as much

So far my only gripe is that nearing end-game that the bosses are unforgiving and tanky af with damage that if aimed at you will melt your health
This requires better armour and better human weapons but is quite a grind for resources to achieve making them. 

Towards the end it becomes a resources farming sim but i guess holds engagement so i can't fault it.

I recommend the game for those like me who thought by Pokemon X and Y, pokemon died and wished they ventured into new genres and gameplay like how they did for mystery dungeon
love me a "grindy game", perfect
repetitive boring grinder

Debates and everything aside, this is a really good game.

I love the open world they have created. I've killed many hours in this game hunting down the pals, hitting the towers, and trying to complete all of the achievements. They've done some wicked cool collabs and have been slowly making Updates that I personally really like. The intro of coloring some structure items (wood, stone, glass, etc) was really fun. Fun little side note, if you paint glass "Black" it almost looks completely clear, makes for great floor to ceiling windows! I also really liked the addition of the flipped triangle wall. Once good only for roofs, now lets you make some fun alternative walls. I've been having a lot of fun with this.

I will admit the game CAN be a bit of a grind, you do need to constantly be gathering items, and the higher your level gets the longer it takes to level up. Several of the bosses are nearly impossible without upgrading gear and pals and working on battle stats, but it IS possible. And thankfully as far as items go, your pals can help you with gathering certain ones on base!

As far as the towers go, if you aren't equipped properly it can and will get frustrating quickly. Pal typing can help a lot, levels help a lot, but the right gear, and items will help the most. Playing with others can and will help a lot, so don't be afraid to poke your friends. The more people and pals you have at your disposal the easier I feel it will be.

Overall the game has a lot of different mechanics that really fits the open world survival craft theme, and I couldn't be happier, and they keep updating periodically making it just a little better each time. I've been enjoying it. It may not be for folks who can't stand any kind of grind in a game, but its a great starter if you wanna get a base builder friend/partner into it and get them into their first "survival" game with a bunch of cute critters or badass guns and armor sets. Hell maybe they like both!

With all of the achievements out of the way for me, I am looking forward to there maybe being more, but it's not required, I recently decided I am going to breed out all the pals, which will take some grinding mind you... and max out the stars on every single one. Not once, but Twice. One of each gender. Why? Because I feel like it, and it gives me something I can do while still playing with friends who haven't gotten through everything yet. I actually really enjoy having this as a simple task where I can still set it aside and go do other things.

I have probably built bases in well over 50 locations now between the different games I've played with friends and my boyfriend. I love overhauling my current base in my finished game to redesign everything and see what's new with updates. Modifying the layout of the house, fitting all the crafting things I need within, using random items to try and create things that aren't yet in the game. It's a lot of fun and I really recommend branching out and trying all kinds of things. Unlocking all the neat items to build with, but even without them there are a lot of cool things you can do with the basics.

I found the "wooden" defense walls were too tall and kinda ugly, so one time I used a bunch of the wooden Pillars/columns and spent probably too much time lining them all up beside each other on the ground to make a fence, the bonus to this? A Rounded Fence instead of the typical squares/corners. It looks really nice and you can place small objects on top of them as well, like the little square lanterns and such.

To the things gamers care more about sometimes:
I have very rarely run into glitches. Back when I first started playing you could easily clip through walls and fall through the world. While it's still possible, the odds of it happening now are extremely low.

Multiplayer or single player, there is a lot to do either way. You can have different guilds if you don't wanna share a space with friends and have a little competition, or you can all be in the same guild together and tackle everything together, whatever you vibe with!

Stamina is a Pain. It drains quickly and if you don't add to it everything feels like that much more of a grind, I focused mostly on increasing that and weight capacity for items, saving health and other stats for when potions were unlocked later in the game since most of those can also be increased with armor (health/defense especially)

Respawn: If you get stuck anywhere, you can pick which of your bases OR the main starting area's, to spawn back to. Something to note is to check your game settings before playing. There are options for a "hard" run where you lose everything when you respawn. Pals/Items/etc. Or an easy version where you don't lose anything. Depending on your vibe, make sure to choose accordingly. Losing everything if you don't have the means to obtain things quickly can set you back a fair amount.

Speed Runs: It is technically possible to speed through a lot if you play a custom game. You can get Egg hatch times down to literally nothing, increase exp gain and the like to try and push it along faster, etc. The game devs really put a lot of thought into making sure there was something for everyone, and made it as easy as possible to access. Shout out to them for thinking of these things~ You can also adjust the length of night and day to suit your needs. I haven't found much yet that they haven't thought of.

Terarria pals, they do not count towards your palpedia, you can complete it without having all the terarria pals so don't sweat it. They are super cool however, and the bats if you have them in your part will just chill outside with you even if you don't have them as your active pal as far as I have seen so far. I pulled together a full party of them just to see what would happen, and all of them were flying around following me. There are several little surprises like that around.

It would be fun if they added some more customization options for the players themselves, but not mandatory. More hair styles would be neat, maybe options for scars for fun. They already have a lot of neat little accessories and the option to layer them over your armor and such which is nice too. So play around with options if you wanna~

If you don't wanna play solo?? For any of my friendo's who have this one on their wishlist or were thinking of maybe trying it. Download it. Poke me, I'll play anytime I'm free~ Whether its a new world or an old one. I genuinely just really enjoy this game.

"Pokemon with guns" This game is so much more than that, and I hope more people take the time to actually explore it and see all these things for themselves~

If I could leave a neutral review, I would. Palworld is essentialy like Pokemon Legends Arceus mixed with Valheim building mechanics, breath of the wild graphics and fortnite combat. Its a good game in many regards, but then theres elements that hold it back significantly.

The Pros:
+ Weaker pals can be powered up and you could keep them till the end of the game if you like while still being useful (eg- I had a Direhowl I kept with me).
+ You can essentially teach any pal any move from any element, while being alot less restricted. You still benefit from having abilities that boost the power of moves that match the pals typing. 
+ The world feels big with initially always something to do (eg- mining, finding items, opening chests, fighting npcs, capturing new pals, finding statues etc) with wide variations of interesting terrain.
+ The Pal Sphere capture mechanics is fair and well done, which you can see the capture rate and how successful a throw was, which is a huge improvement of other systems ive seen in other games.
+ NPCs respawn in the world if you kill or capture one, meaning your not locked out from speaking to them/content.
+ Fast travel is well implemented.
+ Its really cool having pals actively working at base, where they dont just sit in a box where you forget about them.
+ Levels increase depending on the area youre exploring and you can explore anywhere right off the bat.
+ A wide number of customisable options available for customising a worlds gameplay (eg- I had more objects spawn when mining/looting that reduces grind and you can change the difficulty too).
+ Different pals have regional variants (usually just a colour change/typing change though). Id still rather more pal species variety however but its still a cool addition.
+ It has multiplayer!

The Negatives:
- Its an extremely grindy game to the point of it not being fun. Its like those mobile games where you need to sit there waiting for something to craft and you need to make an item, to get another item, to craft this item before making the item you were originally trying to make. Its a whole frustrating process that signficantly brings down the game.
- Theres no in game weather like rain/strong wind/snow/dust storms.
-the police mechanic is terrible, where they will keep spawning in randomly out of thin air just to pursue you endlessly.
- Exploring caves/raiding bases feels way too repetitive. It gets really boring.
- Theres next to no in game music that massively breaks immersion.
- The radius for where you can build in a base feels way too small and you cant label them on the map.
- Armor sets/weapon/accessory variety is very limited. 
- Combat is passable but feels very clunky compared to many other games.
- The carry weight and weapon durabiliy mechanic is on by default (can be turned off in world settings)
- Buildings scattered around the world lack enough variety.
- Captured humans are very useless. 
- Lucky Pals (they just have better abilities and are bigger in size) dont feel rewarding to farm compared to pokemons shiny hunting.
- Legendary Pals are stationary and should roam the world. It feels like capturing any other pal.
- Mid to late game, exploring the world for the same chests, the same random items on the ground, the same ores, it gets boring, 
- Not being able to remap all controls such as mouse scroll thats permanently set to weapon toggle. 

Palworld is still in early access, with some amazing ideas and so much potential, but theres too many things holding it back for me to recommend at this present time. Don't go in expecting it to be just "Pokemon with Guns" as Palworld is its own unique experience. For me, its currently a neutral rating but hopefully they address these issues going into the full release.

Palworld is a nice little game. It's ARK with Pokemon. I bought the game because I heard many great things about it, and I remember people loving the game when it first launched.

Unfortunately, I have quite a few problems with the game. IMO there are a lot of baffling decisions when it comes to the difficulty, even if "oh it's fine though cause you can change it at will". I find this is still worthy of criticism because the default settings themselves don't feel balanced, so you need to tweak things slowly until you find what works for you. That, or you look up an online guide that says "these are the best palworld settings for maximum fun!" but fun is subjective...

The music is perfectly mediocre. Not much else to say about it lol

Quests are a nice little checkmark, but the lack of any real interconnected quest or side story makes it feel empty. It would've been nice if the game had quest chains with written stories, but nah, they're all individual.

The "main story" or "main objective" to defeat the towers is... serviceable. The unfortunate thing is that the game doesn't eventually develop into a mood of "you're now in the final area! You've almost beaten the game!". Things just feel the same, all the time. The only difference is that now you can unlock things in the upgrade tree... yay? I've yet to defeat the Feybreak Tower, but part of me believes me that the game will not have any closure or sense of finality once I do that. You may argue that the game is still in early access.. but c'mon. That's just cope.

The combat itself is really whatever. Player interactivity feels practically absent: Because Player weapons feel so weak in general, the only thing you're left to do in combat is use items, switch Pals, and dodge attacks. This makes Palworld feel more like an auto-clicker/afk.

One more problem I can point out is the grinding is so monotonous in the late game. Yes, change the settings... But if your stuff is underleveled, there's isn't a fun way to level up your Pals if you're trying to get from 60 to 65. This problem is highlighted even more when you realize the combat is just a dodge/switch Pal loop.

--

For the positives though, and why Palworld may be good for you..
- You like optimizing teams
- You like exploring
- The game genuinely feels like an "Open world Pokemon game" at times
- You like breeding
- You like collecting 
- You like optimizing workflow in a factory setting

To conclude, I've had my fun with the game, but I find it hard to believe there isn't a better game out there than what Palworld has to offer. The fact that I got mislead by the people who called it "Pokemon with guns" when it's actually "ARK with a pokemon mod" feels saddening, because I would've thought the game had more stuff to do with the Pals other than labor.  

You could say that "Palworld just isn't for me", and that might be true. I do like my games to have a sense of direction and finality to them, like how you can still explore and do things in Elden Ring after you've defeated the final boss (which you know is the final boss due to the setup).

At the very least, in terms of the Price per Playtime, Palworld is definitely worth buying.

Palworld in its current form is a good game, but not great. It can get there with the release of 1.0, but it's not there yet.

The gunplay loses its lustre very fast and the pal fighting is just okay. Being able to customise world settings allows you to get rid of things you don't like and increase things that you do like. The base building is the main selling part of the game, but still needs a lot more polish. The guys you can catch are all very varied and neat, and the breeding/IV process is straightforward. There is a fair amount of grinding in the late-game, but if you like that, then it won't bother you.

The game is fine solo, but I would recommend playing it with friends, as it truly shines in its multiplayer and feels more lived-in as a result. With friends, it also makes the variety of combat-related things to do more interesting as well, like the enemy bases, dungeons, towers, and raids. Friends cannot help with the poorly designed oil rigs, though, as I think that all of the oil rigs probably require an overhaul to actually be fun to go through.

I have high hopes for Palworld's future as 1.0 comes ever closer. It won't ever be the Pokémon killer that people initially lauded it to be, but it doesn't have to be. It can carve out its own niche just fine, and I look forward to seeing how it turns out at full release.

Research technologies as you/pals level up. that help you, Pals and your tech advance. Create multiple bases (as complex or basic as you want) filled with your Pals that do a lot of the busy work for you (mine/farm/make items etc).

The game ran smoothly on high/epic settings. The graphics looked good, in that they fit the game style.  Some lower res textures did not bother me. The in game music is really good without feeling repetitive, even after 300 hrs.

The game also has a lot of options (can change whenever you load up) to help you enjoy the game you want (no base raids, less stamina drop etc). There is also a great modding community for even more choice.

Looking forward to a further playthrough when the game comes out of early access.

Until you reach the 5th/6th tower the game is a lot of fun. After that it becomes an absolute slog of grinding and frustration. Mid to late game bosses have way too much health and deal way too much damage to the point where if you bring a full team that counters the bosses element they will be one shot anyway. Materials needed for late game gear have absurd crafting costs that take way too long farm for (the strongest weapon currently is not even worth crafting because the amount of materials needed to craft the ammo is ridiculous). I wish I could recommend it as the first half of the game is genuinely well paced but after that it just becomes monotonous.

Imagine Necesse grew up, moved to a tropical island, and started a full-scale manufacturing empire. The transition to 3D makes the base automation feel incredibly satisfying—there’s nothing quite like watching a giant electric sheep handle your logistics while you oversee the production lines.

The Loop: From "Aww" to "Get Back to Work"
The early game is pure magic. You’re exploring, capturing, and marveling at the physics of it all. However, the mid-to-late game for a solo player hits a familiar, daunting wall. Your base becomes a well-oiled machine, the gameplay loop locks in, and suddenly you realize you’ve accidentally become a middle manager for magical creatures. Just when you think you're done, the arenas and massive endgame scale pull you back into the chaos.

The Wall: A Disrespectful Grind
Despite the addictive "just five more minutes" quality, the endgame reveals a brutal mathematical reality that is fundamentally disrespectful of a human's time. To field a competitive raid team of 15 Pals at 4 stars, you are looking at a minimum of 1,740+ Pals bred or captured just for the condensation process.

When you factor in the hunt for "God Roll" passive traits, a solo player is forced into a staggering cycle of:

Managing hundreds of breeding eggs.

Discarding/butchering thousands of "failed" units.

Engaging in what eventually feels like unpaid data entry.

For a soloist, this isn't a test of skill; it's a test of sheer endurance that the game doesn't sufficiently reward.

This does not include end game farming, ingots or ore introduced in Feybreak.. its just insanity, I mean I earn a decent real life salary for what feels like less work than this. If you play vanilla, without mods, Wand or any custom world settings, you will be crucified!

The Verdict: To 100%, I cant recommend this game at all, its far too involved.
Palworld is a brilliant, genre-bending masterpiece for the first 40 hours, but it eventually transforms into a repetitive grind that treats the player like a machine, the time investment (even when factoring in sliders or world adjustments) is just beyond acceptable and because the endgame requirements are so wildly out of sync with a solo player's time, the sheer scale and chaos - while addictive - can't quite save it from feeling like a second job. 

It’s a superb experience that ultimately demands more than it gives back.

However, in reality, it brings nothing new into the survival genre itself. People who are coming from the survival genre will find the exact same reskin of concepts of grind and automation. Adventure is meh as the world itself is empty and bland. Dungeons are boring, and everything else about the game including quests are undercooked except the Pal and automation mechanics. This game will offer you nothing new, if you are coming from other survival games and looking for a game that does different within the genre. If you are a monster collector fan, game will feel fun until you hit bigger grinds that are part of survival games and you might be turned off by it.

Just got the 57/57 achievements as of May 5, 2026.  So, I think I can give a full review.

I returned to this game after a two year break--I basically jumped on the hype train when it came out, and then it faded away.  It felt very early access back then.

Returning to this game in 2026, the polish is there and this game shines. It works for both casual players and those who want to min-max.  The QoL improvements from when I last played are staggering. One includes a chest that allows you to store a set number of items that are shared amongst bases--similar to an Ender Chest from Minecraft.  While I'm not sure if this was present in 2024, the global palbox prevents you from running out of storage with your Pals--provided you do not breed 10,000. Expanded machinery and a money printer later in the game really help you keep the pace of this game.

That's where I heap on the praise.  A lot of survival games have a great start, and a terrible middle, and a lackluster end. I'm looking at you, Studio Wildcard.  Palworld has masterful pacing in a survival crafting game.  There is some RNG, but it's not abominable and you can beat the bosses and the game without having to grind for the absolutely best gear.  The additions of Sukijima--which added Japanese architecture, is a crown jewel of this game that does indeed have an Albernueric farm.

I was watching a guy review Warhammer 40k, and he said "When something becomes derivative enough, it eventually swings back to being original."  That's how this game feels.  You have things torn from other games like:

- Evergaols, spirit springs and somehow Godfrey from Elden Ring
- the obvious monster catching that Nintendon't ripped off from another game
- the crafting mechanics of Ark without the janky build mechanics
- the BotW scope of the map
- odd lore that somehow feels like it was inspired by Adventure Time?

But when you mix all these ingredients together, you get a great game.  Some games, when I finish them, I feel glad it's over.  This is one of those games where I'm glad it existed.

The premise i quite enjoyed. I was an avid Pokemon player from Diamond to White to Pokemon Y.

To me there is two sides of this fence, me who was a Pokemon fan and was tired after Pokemon Y that they were releasing the same formula year after year where most of us wanted pokemon in a different genre.
To me, Palworld scratched that itch like Pixelmon did where Gamefreak didn't dare to adventure. On the other side is my friend who like me was brought up with Pokemon but due to the nature of it being Chinese owned, in their eyes a "blatant ripoff" and the Pals are unoriginal/uninteresting....

To me, i enjoy the pals, some are like early level pokemon like bidoof and pidgey, some appear as pseudo-bosses.

The gameplay is refreshing, gliding around the map until you can get a pal that can fly quickly. Mixture of using a Pal to fight and using your own weapons to fight off wild pals. Some Pals are passive unless attacked and some attack on sight.

There are 120 or so pals, some are "ignis" varients usually mirroring the type, so if one was blue coloured and ice type, the other is pink and fire type and i quite like this for in my mind "shinies with type variation"
On Shinies, there are ones that glow in the wild, often shining and being overleveled from their normal level. There are also pseudo-bosses of normal pals you can get that are "oversized" which is cool too.

The building mechanic exists, i haven't used it for base building all that much but seems cool if not a bit weird when the ground is uneven. 

The crafting for foods, resource refining, etc is quite entertaining to make an efficient workhorse of a base with work pals.

Leveling up it doesn't explain that catching 10-20 of new pals gives you a massive exp boost that fighting doesn't deliver as much

So far my only gripe is that nearing end-game that the bosses are unforgiving and tanky af with damage that if aimed at you will melt your health
This requires better armour and better human weapons but is quite a grind for resources to achieve making them. 

Towards the end it becomes a resources farming sim but i guess holds engagement so i can't fault it.

I recommend the game for those like me who thought by Pokemon X and Y, pokemon died and wished they ventured into new genres and gameplay like how they did for mystery dungeon
love me a "grindy game", perfect
repetitive boring grinder

Debates and everything aside, this is a really good game.

I love the open world they have created. I've killed many hours in this game hunting down the pals, hitting the towers, and trying to complete all of the achievements. They've done some wicked cool collabs and have been slowly making Updates that I personally really like. The intro of coloring some structure items (wood, stone, glass, etc) was really fun. Fun little side note, if you paint glass "Black" it almost looks completely clear, makes for great floor to ceiling windows! I also really liked the addition of the flipped triangle wall. Once good only for roofs, now lets you make some fun alternative walls. I've been having a lot of fun with this.

I will admit the game CAN be a bit of a grind, you do need to constantly be gathering items, and the higher your level gets the longer it takes to level up. Several of the bosses are nearly impossible without upgrading gear and pals and working on battle stats, but it IS possible. And thankfully as far as items go, your pals can help you with gathering certain ones on base!

As far as the towers go, if you aren't equipped properly it can and will get frustrating quickly. Pal typing can help a lot, levels help a lot, but the right gear, and items will help the most. Playing with others can and will help a lot, so don't be afraid to poke your friends. The more people and pals you have at your disposal the easier I feel it will be.

Overall the game has a lot of different mechanics that really fits the open world survival craft theme, and I couldn't be happier, and they keep updating periodically making it just a little better each time. I've been enjoying it. It may not be for folks who can't stand any kind of grind in a game, but its a great starter if you wanna get a base builder friend/partner into it and get them into their first "survival" game with a bunch of cute critters or badass guns and armor sets. Hell maybe they like both!

With all of the achievements out of the way for me, I am looking forward to there maybe being more, but it's not required, I recently decided I am going to breed out all the pals, which will take some grinding mind you... and max out the stars on every single one. Not once, but Twice. One of each gender. Why? Because I feel like it, and it gives me something I can do while still playing with friends who haven't gotten through everything yet. I actually really enjoy having this as a simple task where I can still set it aside and go do other things.

I have probably built bases in well over 50 locations now between the different games I've played with friends and my boyfriend. I love overhauling my current base in my finished game to redesign everything and see what's new with updates. Modifying the layout of the house, fitting all the crafting things I need within, using random items to try and create things that aren't yet in the game. It's a lot of fun and I really recommend branching out and trying all kinds of things. Unlocking all the neat items to build with, but even without them there are a lot of cool things you can do with the basics.

I found the "wooden" defense walls were too tall and kinda ugly, so one time I used a bunch of the wooden Pillars/columns and spent probably too much time lining them all up beside each other on the ground to make a fence, the bonus to this? A Rounded Fence instead of the typical squares/corners. It looks really nice and you can place small objects on top of them as well, like the little square lanterns and such.

To the things gamers care more about sometimes:
I have very rarely run into glitches. Back when I first started playing you could easily clip through walls and fall through the world. While it's still possible, the odds of it happening now are extremely low.

Multiplayer or single player, there is a lot to do either way. You can have different guilds if you don't wanna share a space with friends and have a little competition, or you can all be in the same guild together and tackle everything together, whatever you vibe with!

Stamina is a Pain. It drains quickly and if you don't add to it everything feels like that much more of a grind, I focused mostly on increasing that and weight capacity for items, saving health and other stats for when potions were unlocked later in the game since most of those can also be increased with armor (health/defense especially)

Respawn: If you get stuck anywhere, you can pick which of your bases OR the main starting area's, to spawn back to. Something to note is to check your game settings before playing. There are options for a "hard" run where you lose everything when you respawn. Pals/Items/etc. Or an easy version where you don't lose anything. Depending on your vibe, make sure to choose accordingly. Losing everything if you don't have the means to obtain things quickly can set you back a fair amount.

Speed Runs: It is technically possible to speed through a lot if you play a custom game. You can get Egg hatch times down to literally nothing, increase exp gain and the like to try and push it along faster, etc. The game devs really put a lot of thought into making sure there was something for everyone, and made it as easy as possible to access. Shout out to them for thinking of these things~ You can also adjust the length of night and day to suit your needs. I haven't found much yet that they haven't thought of.

Terarria pals, they do not count towards your palpedia, you can complete it without having all the terarria pals so don't sweat it. They are super cool however, and the bats if you have them in your part will just chill outside with you even if you don't have them as your active pal as far as I have seen so far. I pulled together a full party of them just to see what would happen, and all of them were flying around following me. There are several little surprises like that around.

If I could leave a neutral review, I would. Palworld is essentialy like Pokemon Legends Arceus mixed with Valheim building mechanics, breath of the wild graphics and fortnite combat. Its a good game in many regards, but then theres elements that hold it back significantly.

The Pros:
+ Weaker pals can be powered up and you could keep them till the end of the game if you like while still being useful (eg- I had a Direhowl I kept with me).
+ You can essentially teach any pal any move from any element, while being alot less restricted. You still benefit from having abilities that boost the power of moves that match the pals typing. 
+ The world feels big with initially always something to do (eg- mining, finding items, opening chests, fighting npcs, capturing new pals, finding statues etc) with wide variations of interesting terrain.
+ The Pal Sphere capture mechanics is fair and well done, which you can see the capture rate and how successful a throw was, which is a huge improvement of other systems ive seen in other games.
+ NPCs respawn in the world if you kill or capture one, meaning your not locked out from speaking to them/content.
+ Fast travel is well implemented.
+ Its really cool having pals actively working at base, where they dont just sit in a box where you forget about them.
+ Levels increase depending on the area youre exploring and you can explore anywhere right off the bat.
+ A wide number of customisable options available for customising a worlds gameplay (eg- I had more objects spawn when mining/looting that reduces grind and you can change the difficulty too).
+ Different pals have regional variants (usually just a colour change/typing change though). Id still rather more pal species variety however but its still a cool addition.
+ It has multiplayer!

The Negatives:
- Its an extremely grindy game to the point of it not being fun. Its like those mobile games where you need to sit there waiting for something to craft and you need to make an item, to get another item, to craft this item before making the item you were originally trying to make. Its a whole frustrating process that signficantly brings down the game.
- Theres no in game weather like rain/strong wind/snow/dust storms.
-the police mechanic is terrible, where they will keep spawning in randomly out of thin air just to pursue you endlessly.
- Exploring caves/raiding bases feels way too repetitive. It gets really boring.
- Theres next to no in game music that massively breaks immersion.
- The radius for where you can build in a base feels way too small and you cant label them on the map.
- Armor sets/weapon/accessory variety is very limited. 
- Combat is passable but feels very clunky compared to many other games.
- The carry weight and weapon durabiliy mechanic is on by default (can be turned off in world settings)
- Buildings scattered around the world lack enough variety.
- Captured humans are very useless. 
- Lucky Pals (they just have better abilities and are bigger in size) dont feel rewarding to farm compared to pokemons shiny hunting.
- Legendary Pals are stationary and should roam the world. It feels like capturing any other pal.
- Mid to late game, exploring the world for the same chests, the same random items on the ground, the same ores, it gets boring, 
- Not being able to remap all controls such as mouse scroll thats permanently set to weapon toggle. 

Palworld is still in early access, with some amazing ideas and so much potential, but theres too many things holding it back for me to recommend at this present time. Don't go in expecting it to be just "Pokemon with Guns" as Palworld is its own unique experience. For me, its currently a neutral rating but hopefully they address these issues going into the full release.

Palworld is a nice little game. It's ARK with Pokemon. I bought the game because I heard many great things about it, and I remember people loving the game when it first launched.

Unfortunately, I have quite a few problems with the game. IMO there are a lot of baffling decisions when it comes to the difficulty, even if "oh it's fine though cause you can change it at will". I find this is still worthy of criticism because the default settings themselves don't feel balanced, so you need to tweak things slowly until you find what works for you. That, or you look up an online guide that says "these are the best palworld settings for maximum fun!" but fun is subjective...

The music is perfectly mediocre. Not much else to say about it lol

Quests are a nice little checkmark, but the lack of any real interconnected quest or side story makes it feel empty. It would've been nice if the game had quest chains with written stories, but nah, they're all individual.

The "main story" or "main objective" to defeat the towers is... serviceable. The unfortunate thing is that the game doesn't eventually develop into a mood of "you're now in the final area! You've almost beaten the game!". Things just feel the same, all the time. The only difference is that now you can unlock things in the upgrade tree... yay? I've yet to defeat the Feybreak Tower, but part of me believes me that the game will not have any closure or sense of finality once I do that. You may argue that the game is still in early access.. but c'mon. That's just cope.

The combat itself is really whatever. Player interactivity feels practically absent: Because Player weapons feel so weak in general, the only thing you're left to do in combat is use items, switch Pals, and dodge attacks. This makes Palworld feel more like an auto-clicker/afk.

One more problem I can point out is the grinding is so monotonous in the late game. Yes, change the settings... But if your stuff is underleveled, there's isn't a fun way to level up your Pals if you're trying to get from 60 to 65. This problem is highlighted even more when you realize the combat is just a dodge/switch Pal loop.

--

For the positives though, and why Palworld may be good for you..
- You like optimizing teams
- You like exploring
- The game genuinely feels like an "Open world Pokemon game" at times
- You like breeding
- You like collecting 
- You like optimizing workflow in a factory setting

To conclude, I've had my fun with the game, but I find it hard to believe there isn't a better game out there than what Palworld has to offer. The fact that I got mislead by the people who called it "Pokemon with guns" when it's actually "ARK with a pokemon mod" feels saddening, because I would've thought the game had more stuff to do with the Pals other than labor.  

You could say that "Palworld just isn't for me", and that might be true. I do like my games to have a sense of direction and finality to them, like how you can still explore and do things in Elden Ring after you've defeated the final boss (which you know is the final boss due to the setup).

At the very least, in terms of the Price per Playtime, Palworld is definitely worth buying.

Palworld in its current form is a good game, but not great. It can get there with the release of 1.0, but it's not there yet.

The gunplay loses its lustre very fast and the pal fighting is just okay. Being able to customise world settings allows you to get rid of things you don't like and increase things that you do like. The base building is the main selling part of the game, but still needs a lot more polish. The guys you can catch are all very varied and neat, and the breeding/IV process is straightforward. There is a fair amount of grinding in the late-game, but if you like that, then it won't bother you.

The game is fine solo, but I would recommend playing it with friends, as it truly shines in its multiplayer and feels more lived-in as a result. With friends, it also makes the variety of combat-related things to do more interesting as well, like the enemy bases, dungeons, towers, and raids. Friends cannot help with the poorly designed oil rigs, though, as I think that all of the oil rigs probably require an overhaul to actually be fun to go through.

I have high hopes for Palworld's future as 1.0 comes ever closer. It won't ever be the Pokémon killer that people initially lauded it to be, but it doesn't have to be. It can carve out its own niche just fine, and I look forward to seeing how it turns out at full release.

Been enjoying for 100's of hours single player. Big open world to explore on foot, riding/flying pals and gliding/grappling/climbing across diverse landscapes.

Research technologies as you/pals level up. that help you, Pals and your tech advance. Create multiple bases (as complex or basic as you want) filled with your Pals that do a lot of the busy work for you (mine/farm/make items etc).

The game ran smoothly on high/epic settings. The graphics looked good, in that they fit the game style.  Some lower res textures did not bother me. The in game music is really good without feeling repetitive, even after 300 hrs.

The game also has a lot of options (can change whenever you load up) to help you enjoy the game you want (no base raids, less stamina drop etc). There is also a great modding community for even more choice.

Looking forward to a further playthrough when the game comes out of early access.

Until you reach the 5th/6th tower the game is a lot of fun. After that it becomes an absolute slog of grinding and frustration. Mid to late game bosses have way too much health and deal way too much damage to the point where if you bring a full team that counters the bosses element they will be one shot anyway. Materials needed for late game gear have absurd crafting costs that take way too long farm for (the strongest weapon currently is not even worth crafting because the amount of materials needed to craft the ammo is ridiculous). I wish I could recommend it as the first half of the game is genuinely well paced but after that it just becomes monotonous.

Imagine Necesse grew up, moved to a tropical island, and started a full-scale manufacturing empire. The transition to 3D makes the base automation feel incredibly satisfying—there’s nothing quite like watching a giant electric sheep handle your logistics while you oversee the production lines.

The Loop: From "Aww" to "Get Back to Work"
The early game is pure magic. You’re exploring, capturing, and marveling at the physics of it all. However, the mid-to-late game for a solo player hits a familiar, daunting wall. Your base becomes a well-oiled machine, the gameplay loop locks in, and suddenly you realize you’ve accidentally become a middle manager for magical creatures. Just when you think you're done, the arenas and massive endgame scale pull you back into the chaos.

The Wall: A Disrespectful Grind
Despite the addictive "just five more minutes" quality, the endgame reveals a brutal mathematical reality that is fundamentally disrespectful of a human's time. To field a competitive raid team of 15 Pals at 4 stars, you are looking at a minimum of 1,740+ Pals bred or captured just for the condensation process.

When you factor in the hunt for "God Roll" passive traits, a solo player is forced into a staggering cycle of:

Managing hundreds of breeding eggs.

Discarding/butchering thousands of "failed" units.

Engaging in what eventually feels like unpaid data entry.

For a soloist, this isn't a test of skill; it's a test of sheer endurance that the game doesn't sufficiently reward.

This does not include end game farming, ingots or ore introduced in Feybreak.. its just insanity, I mean I earn a decent real life salary for what feels like less work than this. If you play vanilla, without mods, Wand or any custom world settings, you will be crucified!

The Verdict: To 100%, I cant recommend this game at all, its far too involved.
Palworld is a brilliant, genre-bending masterpiece for the first 40 hours, but it eventually transforms into a repetitive grind that treats the player like a machine, the time investment (even when factoring in sliders or world adjustments) is just beyond acceptable and because the endgame requirements are so wildly out of sync with a solo player's time, the sheer scale and chaos - while addictive - can't quite save it from feeling like a second job. 

It’s a superb experience that ultimately demands more than it gives back.

Very fun, entertainingly quirky. It's like a love-letter to all the survival/building games out there, and honestly for all the obvious appearance comparisons out there...

This game feels like the devs played Ark and Conan Exiles, got frustrated with the mechanics in those, and said they could do better. And in general they did. For all the "off-brand Pokewhatever" vibe, I pick up a lot of the Pals being far more reminiscent of some of the mid-to-endgame dinos from Ark (Reptyro, I'm lookin' at you, you're the Ankylo's anime cousin.)

This is perfectly playable in early access, and they keep adding free content -- collabs and additions that aren't doing that "pay for this DLC before the game is out of early access" nonsense thus far.

It runs far better than and has way less jank in base building than Ark EVER HAS. Thus far my experience is the bugs are few and far between. The dungeons and boss instances are a little repetitive but fun.

The only real issue I have is while working through the main quest sometimes there's a real jump, you get told "go beat this boss in this place" and they're waaaaay tougher than the previous boss, so you have to take time and build up when you didn't expect it.

I wish there was a Mixed review. Palworld is a survival game with a skin of a Monster Collector. It's a nice fresh breath of air for people playing for the Monster Collector genre aspect, It got a lot of traction because people got tired of same old Pokemon formula in Monster collector genre. However, in reality, it brings nothing new into the survival genre itself. People who are coming from the survival genre will find the exact same reskin of concepts of grind and automation. Adventure is meh as the world itself is empty and bland. Dungeons are boring, and everything else about the game including quests are undercooked except the Pal and automation mechanics. Same survival dna where grind gets worse and more time-consuming near-endgame. This game will offer you nothing new, if you are coming from other survival games and looking for a game that does different within the genre. If you are a monster collector fan, game will feel fun until you hit bigger grinds that are part of survival games and you might be turned off by it.
